topic: Iterators - Converting to and from Streams

turns.content: Hey, I wrote a sync iterator that returns files, but I want to use it in an async context. I wrapped the result of my iterator with stream::iter inside a Box<dyn Stream<Item = FileResult> + Send> and then called collect::<Vec<_>>(). Is this the idiomatic way to bridge sync iterators with async streams?

You are on the right track using stream::iter, but there is a more ergonomic approach. Instead of manually boxing your iterator into a trait object, you should use IntoStream which lets you convert any sync iterator directly into an AsyncIterator without needing to implement Stream traits yourself.
Oh right, I didn't consider that. So if I have my FileSyncIter struct with a next method returning Option<Result<File>>, can I just call .into_stream() on it?

Exactly. Your FileSyncIter implements std::iter::Iterator, so you can simply invoke .into_stream() to get an AsyncStream<Item = Result<File>>. This avoids the need for Box<dyn Stream> and keeps your code type-safe without dynamic dispatch.
That sounds much cleaner. But what if I want to consume this stream inside a tokio::spawn task? Do I still need to wrap the IntoStream result in something else?

No, you can spawn it directly since IntoStream implements Send and Sync. Inside your spawned task, you will call .collect::<Vec<_>>() or use await on each item depending on whether you need to process them as they arrive or collect them first.
Got it. But I am worried about the error handling. If one file fails, does the whole stream stop? Should I use .into_stream() with a custom error type instead of Result?

The default behavior is to propagate errors through the Result item, so a single failure will cause that specific iteration to fail unless you handle it. If you want more control over error propagation, you can implement IntoStream for your type with a custom error type using std::future::ready or std::pin::Pin.

Actually, I realized my FileSyncIter doesn't have the right trait bounds. It implements Iterator but not Send because it holds a local reference to the file system. Should I change it to Box<dyn FnMut() -> Option<Result<File>> + Send>?

You do not need to use Box<dyn FnMut>. Your FileSyncIter can hold an Arc<RefCell<...>> or a static reference if it is safe. The key is ensuring that the iterator's state is Send so that IntoStream works correctly with tokio::spawn.
I see. So I should update FileSyncIter to store an Arc<RefCell<Vec<File>>> instead of a raw Vec? That way it becomes Send and Sync?

Yes, using Arc<RefCell> is a common pattern for sharing mutable state across threads. However, note that if you only need to send the iterator itself, you might not even need RefCell depending on how much mutability your iterator requires.
Wait, I am getting confused about when to use IntoStream vs manual Stream implementation. Is there a rule of thumb?

Use IntoStream whenever your iterator already implements std::iter::Iterator and you want to convert it to an async stream quickly. Only implement the full Stream trait manually if you need custom buffering, specific error handling, or complex state management that standard iterators cannot provide.
That makes sense. One last thing, can I convert an AsyncIterator back to a sync iterator? Like, take the stream from IntoStream and get a regular Iterator?

You cannot directly convert an AsyncIterator into a plain std::iter::Iterator because async code runs in different contexts. You can collect items into a Vec while running the async task, then iterate over that Vec synchronously afterward.

topic: Iterators - Converting to and from Streams

turns.content: Hey, I'm trying to migrate some of our legacy sync iterator logic into async streams but I keep hitting a wall. My data comes in as an impl Iterator type, specifically something like Vec<u8> with an IntoIterator trait implementation, and I want to feed it directly into tokio::stream::Stream. I tried using stream::iter() on the collection itself thinking it would work like Java's Stream API where you just call .asParallelStream(), but the compiler is screaming about lifetime mismatches or missing Send + Sync bounds. Can someone help me bridge this gap correctly?

Hey there! Welcome to Rust systems programming—it's a bit different from Java in how we handle lifetimes and ownership, but the concepts are similar. The issue is that stream::iter() expects an IntoIterator trait object that implements both Send + Sync bounds automatically for most collections like Vec or Box<dyn Iterator>, but if you're wrapping your iterator in a boxed trait object without explicit bounds, you'll hit these errors. You probably need to use IntoStream explicitly with the correct generic parameters instead of relying on stream::iter() directly on raw iterators.

Ah okay, so I should be using IntoStream? But wait, isn't that just for async streams already? My iterator is fully sync and synchronous. Like in Java I'd just use .parallelStream() on a parallel collection. So why am I needing an adapter here?

Good catch! You're right that IntoStream is primarily for async streams, but the key difference is how Rust handles ownership and lifetimes compared to Java's flexible collections. In Java you can pass any Iterator to Stream API without worrying about explicit bounds checks or lifetime annotations, whereas here every type needs to be explicitly Send + Sync if shared across threads. If your iterator isn't already an AsyncIterator (like impl Stream), then IntoStream will fail unless you wrap it properly first.
So what's the actual pattern? Should I just box my iterator and call IntoStream on that? Is there a specific trait I need to implement?

Exactly! If your sync iterator is already Send + Sync, you can simply use IntoStream::<T>(iter) where T is your boxed type. But if you're hitting lifetime errors, it's because stream::iter() doesn't automatically infer lifetimes like Java does—it requires explicit type parameters. Try wrapping your iterator in Box<dyn Iterator<Item = T>> and ensure the bounds are correct before calling IntoStream.
Wait, so I shouldn't be using stream::iter() at all? What if I just use iter() on my collection instead?

That's a great question! You can definitely use iter() on your Vec or other collections to get an iterator, but the problem is that stream::iter() doesn't accept raw iterators—it only works with IntoIterator types. If you want to convert a sync iterator into a Stream, you need to first ensure it implements IntoStream via Box<dyn Iterator> or use IntoIter explicitly from your collection.
Okay so the flow is: collect -> iter -> IntoStream? But what if my data is already a Stream and I need to convert back to an iterator for some sync processing?

Exactly! That's the other direction too. If you have a tokio::stream::Stream, you can use it into_iter() on each item to get back an Iterator, but remember that Stream items must also be Send + Sync if you want to process them synchronously later. Sometimes people forget this and try to collect async streams into Vec without handling the async nature properly.
Got it. So the key is being explicit about bounds and using IntoStream correctly? What if my iterator yields complex objects with internal state?

In that case, you'll need to ensure those types implement Clone or use a different pattern like wrapping them in a struct that tracks state. Rust doesn't have implicit cloning like Java's Collections framework sometimes does, so explicit control over ownership becomes critical here.
Thanks! One last thing—does this apply to async iterators too? Like if I already have an impl Stream and want to convert it back?

Yes, but you'd use IntoIterator instead of IntoStream for async-to-sync conversion. The trade-off is that converting async streams back to sync iterators can lose some performance benefits unless carefully managed with proper bounds and cloning strategies.
Perfect, so now I know the right pattern. Thanks for walking me through this! Just want to confirm: Box<dyn Iterator> + IntoStream is the go-to solution for most cases?

Yes, that's exactly it for bridging sync iterators into async streams in most scenarios. But always check your bounds and ensure Send + Sync are satisfied before assuming everything will work smoothly.
